Clarity.justification: The paper is exceptionally well-written and logically structured. The introduction clearly motivates the distinction between calibration (ECE) and discriminative quality (AUROC). The methodology section explicitly defines the metrics and statistical tests for each hypothesis (H-E1 to H-M3), making it very easy to follow. The results are presented clearly in tables with confidence intervals and p-values.

Novelty.justification: While prior work has noted that RLHF models are overconfident, this paper provides a novel mechanistic explanation: RLHF causes geometric distortion (disproportionate margin inflation for incorrect predictions) rather than just scalar miscalibration. The application of Murphy's Brier score decomposition to separate refinement from reliability in this context is a creative and original approach.

Soundness.justification: The experimental design is highly rigorous. The authors use appropriate statistical tests (bootstrap CIs, permutation tests) to ensure the significance of their findings. The provided code perfectly matches the results reported in the paper, confirming full reproducibility. The authors also transparently acknowledge the limitations of their study. The only minor weakness is the reliance on a single dataset (MMLU) and only 7B models, which slightly limits the generalizability of the findings.

Significance.justification: The findings have important practical implications for the deployment of trustworthy LLMs. By demonstrating that RLHF degrades the discriminative quality of confidence signals, the paper highlights a critical flaw in standard post-hoc calibration methods like temperature scaling, which only address scalar distortion. This will likely spur new research into calibration-aware RLHF and better post-hoc methods.

strengths: Clear distinction between calibration (ECE) and discriminative quality (AUROC).
Rigorous statistical methodology, including bootstrap CIs and permutation tests.
Multi-faceted analysis of the mechanism (conditional margin inflation, normalized slope attenuation, Brier decomposition).
Code is provided and perfectly reproduces the results in the paper.
weaknesses: Limited experimental scope: only two 7B model families (Qwen and Mistral) were evaluated.
Only one dataset (MMLU) was used for evaluation, limiting the generalizability across different tasks.
The paper claims temperature scaling cannot fix geometric distortion but does not empirically demonstrate this, leaving it as a theoretical implication.
